transcript: Welcome to Lesson 7.2, Understanding Triggers, Actions, and Conditions. In the previous lesson, you got an overview of the GoHighLevel platform and how it connects every part of your marketing automation system. Now we are going to go one level deeper, into the actual logic that powers every workflow you will ever build. By the end of this lesson, you will know exactly what triggers, actions, and conditions are, how they work individually, and most importantly, how they combine to create automation workflows that feel intelligent, personalized, and professionally built. This is the vocabulary of automation. Once you internalize these three concepts, you will be able to read, plan, and build any workflow you encounter. Let's get into it. Before we go deep on each component, let me give you the complete picture. An automation workflow is a set of instructions that tells GoHighLevel what to do, when to do it, and how to respond to different situations. Every single workflow you build, whether it is a simple welcome email or a complex multi-week nurture sequence, is made of exactly the same three building blocks. The trigger is what starts the workflow. It is the event that fires the automation into motion. The action is what the workflow does. It is the task Go High Level performs automatically once triggered. The condition is what the workflow checks. It is the decision point that routes a contact down different paths based on who they are or what they have done. Here is a simple real-world example. Trigger. A new lead submits your landing page form. Action Go high level immediately sends them a welcome email. Condition Two days later, it checks whether they opened that email. If yes, they receive a follow-up offer. If no, they receive an SMS reminder instead. Three components Infinite possibilities That is automation logic. Let's start with triggers, because without a trigger, nothing happens. A trigger is the event that activates a workflow. Think of it like a motion sensor on a light. The moment movement is detected, the light turns on automatically. The movement is the trigger. The light turning on is the action. In Go High Level, triggers come in several categories. Form submission triggers fire when a contact fills out any form. Your landing page opt-in form, a contact form, a booking request. This is the most common trigger for lead generation workflows. Tag-based triggers fire when a specific tag is added to or removed from a contact's record. Because tags are applied automatically by other workflows, this is how you chain multiple workflows together. Appointment triggers fire when someone books a call, confirms their attendance, cancels, or shows up. These power your booking confirmation and reminder sequences. Email event triggers fire based on what a contact does with your emails. They opened it, they clicked a link, or they unsubscribed. These are the foundation of behavior-based personalization. Pipeline stage change triggers fire when a contact moves from one stage of your sales pipeline to another. When a lead moves from new to qualified, for example, a follow-up task workflow can fire automatically. And date or time triggers fire on a specific date on a contact birthday or a set number of days after a specific event Here is the most important rule about triggers. Every workflow starts with exactly one trigger. Without a trigger, the workflow never fires. The trigger is the entry point. It defines who enters the workflow and when. Now let's talk about actions. An action is a task. Go high level performs automatically once the trigger has fired. If the trigger is the alarm clock, the action is everything that happens after you wake up, and the automation handles all of it without you lifting a finger. In go high level, the most common actions you will use are these. Send email executes a specific email to the contact. This can be any email you have pre-built. a welcome message, a nurture email, a promotional offer. Send SMS fires a text message to the contact's phone number. Text messages are used for time-sensitive updates, reminders, and quick engagement nudges. Add or remove tag applies a label to the contact's record. This is one of the most strategically powerful actions because it can trigger other workflows, segment contacts for targeted campaigns, and track behavior across your entire system. Wait pauses the workflow for a set period before the next action executes. You might wait one day, three days, one week, whatever timing serves your strategy. Wait steps are what turn a single trigger into a multi-step, multi-day sequence. Move to Pipeline stage advances the contact in your sales pipeline, updating their position automatically based on behavior rather than manual data entry. Create Task assigns a follow-up action to a specific team member inside GHL. When a hot lead reaches a certain point, the system can automatically create a task for your sales rep to call them. Send internal notification alerts you or your team when a contact takes a key action, like clicking your pricing page or booking a call. You are informed in real time without checking manually. And Webhook sends contact data to an external tool like Zapier, your own CRM, or a custom application. We will cover this in detail in Lesson 7.10. Workflows can contain as many actions as you need, executed in sequence from top to bottom. The order matters. Go high level moves through each action in order, one after another. Here is what this looks like inside go high level. Notice the trigger block at the very top. This is where every workflow begins. In this example, the trigger is a form submission from our contact form. Immediately below it, the action blocks stack in sequence. Action 1 sends the welcome email right away. Action 2 is a wait step. Two days pass before anything else happens. Action 3 sends an SMS follow-up. Each block connects to the next, and GoHighLevel executes them in that exact order for every contact who enters this workflow. This vertical structure, trigger at the top, actions below, is how the workflow builder is organized. You will be working inside this exact interface in lesson 7.3. Now we get to the component that takes your automation from basic to genuinely intelligent. Conditions. A condition is a decision point in your workflow. Go High Level calls this the if block And if you have ever seen a flow chart the diamond shape represents exactly this A condition checks something about the contact and then splits the workflow into two paths A yes path for contacts who meet the condition and a no path for contacts who do not. Why does this matter? Because not every contact who enters your workflow is the same person at the same stage of readiness. Without conditions, every contact gets the exact same experience regardless of what they have done. With conditions, your automation responds differently to different behaviors. Here are three examples that show how powerful this is. Your workflow sends a nurture email and then waits two days. The condition asks, did the contact open that email? If yes, they are engaged. Send them the offer email. If no, they may have missed it. Send them an SMS reminder instead. Another example, the condition checks whether the contact has the tag high interest, meaning they have already visited your pricing page or clicked a key link. If yes, notify your sales team immediately. If no, continue the nurture sequence. A third example, the condition asks whether the contact has booked a call. If yes, send the confirmation and preparation materials. If no, send the booking invitation again with a different angle. Conditions answer one question above all others. Given what we know about this person right now, what should happen next? That ability to respond to individual behavior is what separates amateur automation from professional, conversion-driving systems. And here is the if-slash-else condition block inside GoHighLevel's Workflow Builder. You can see the block positioned after the day-three nurture email action. The condition being checked is whether the contact opened the previous email, yes or no. The Yes branch leads to the offer email. The No branch leads to an SMS reminder. Both branches are clearly visible in the canvas, and go high-level routes each contact automatically based on their behavior. No manual sorting, no checking reports and reassigning. The system does it all. You will configure blocks exactly like this starting in Lesson 7.3, and you will use them extensively in Lesson 7.4 through 7.8. Now let me show you all three components working together in a single, complete workflow so you can see how the full picture comes together. The workflow starts with a trigger. A lead submits the free guide form. That single event activates everything else. Action 1 executes immediately. GoHighLevel sends the welcome email delivering the guide. The contact receives it within seconds of submitting the form. Action 2 waits two days. No messages, no interruptions, just a timed pause that creates natural spacing. Then the condition fires. Did they open the welcome email? This is where the workflow branches. If yes, they are engaged. They receive a follow-up email asking if they are ready for the next step. If no, they may need a nudge. They receive an SMS message asking whether they received their guide. Both branches then converge at action four. A tag called nurtured lead is added to every contact who reaches this point, regardless of which branch they took. That tag can trigger an entirely separate workflow, moving them into the next phase of your nurture system automatically This is the anatomy of professional automation logic One trigger a sequence of actions one or more conditions creating intelligent branches and tags creating connections to future workflows Once you can read a workflow like this, you can build any workflow you encounter. Before we close, let me give you five patterns that appear in almost every system you will build. Think of these as templates for your automation thinking. The lead welcome pattern. Form submitted. Send welcome email. Wait one day. Check if email was opened. Then branch to a nurture email or an SMS reminder. The appointment booking pattern. Appointment booked. Send confirmation email. Wait until 24 hours before. Send SMS Reminder Then check whether they showed up Confirmation Path or Rebooking Path The Tag-Based Segmentation Pattern High Interest Tag Added Notify Sales Team Send Personalized Offer Check if they clicked the offer link Move to Closing Pipeline Stage or Continue Nurture The Re-Engagement Pattern Contact has been inactive for 30 days. Send a win-back email. Check if opened. Resume nurture or send a final SMS. If still no response, tag them as inactive. The post-purchase pattern. Pipeline stage change to 1. Send thank you email. Wait 7 days. Send a review request. Check if review was submitted. Add advocate tag or send a reminder. You will build several of these across Lesson 7.3 through 7.8. Recognizing the pattern before you build makes the entire process faster, cleaner, and more strategic. Let's lock in what you learned in this lesson. A trigger is the event that activates a workflow. Every automation starts with exactly one trigger, such as a form submission, a tag change, an appointment booking, or an email event. An action is the task GoHighLevel performs automatically, sending an email, sending an SMS, adding a tag, waiting a set number of days, or notifying your team. Workflows can have as many actions as needed, executed in sequence. A condition is a decision point, called if-slash-else-in-go-high-level, that checks something about the contact and routes them down a yes-or-no path based on their behavior or data. Together, the trigger defines who enters the workflow and when. Actions define what happens at each step. And conditions make the workflow intelligent by personalizing each contact's path through the sequence. And these three components are the vocabulary of automation. Once you understand them, you can read, plan, and build any workflow you encounter from this module forward. Coming up next is Lesson 7.3, Building Your First Automation Workflow. Now that you understand triggers, actions, and conditions as concepts, the next lesson takes you into go-high level to build a complete workflow from scratch. You will apply all three components together in a real lead welcome sequence, starting from a form submission trigger, moving through a welcome email and a wait step, and branching based on email engagement using an if-slash-else condition. This is where your knowledge becomes hands-on skill inside the platform. Let's continue to Lesson 7.3. Thank you.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a comprehensive overview of the fundamental components of automation, specifically focusing on triggers, actions, and conditions. The title, "Understanding Triggers, Actions, and Conditions," sets the stage for an in-depth exploration of these essential building blocks.

**Key Components:**

*   **Triggers:** These are the events or stimuli that initiate an automation process. They can take various forms, such as user interactions, system notifications, or scheduled tasks.
*   **Actions:** Once a trigger is activated, actions are the specific tasks or operations that are executed in response. These can include sending emails, creating tasks, or updating records.
*   **Conditions:** Conditions are the criteria that determine whether an action should be taken or not. They allow for more complex decision-making within automation workflows, enabling personalized and dynamic responses.

**Visual Representation:**

The image features a diagram illustrating the flow of these components:

1.  **Trigger:** Represented by a yellow box with a lightning bolt icon, triggers serve as the starting point for automation.
2.  **Action:** Symbolized by a blue box with a gear icon, actions are the tasks performed in response to triggers.
3.  **Condition:** Depicted by a purple diamond with a circuit icon, conditions evaluate the context and decide the next course of action, branching into "yes" or "no" paths.

**Learning Objectives:**

The image outlines the key takeaways from this lesson:

*   Understanding what triggers are and their common types in GoHighLevel
*   Learning about actions and their role in executing automation logic
*   Exploring conditions and their function in creating intelligent branching paths
*   Discovering how triggers, actions, and conditions combine to form complete workflows
*   Examining real-world examples of these components working together

**Conclusion:**

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise introduction to the core elements of automation: triggers, actions, and conditions. By understanding how these components interact, individuals can design and implement sophisticated automation workflows that streamline processes and enhance efficiency.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of triggers in automation, highlighting their role in initiating workflows. The title "Triggers: What Starts an Automation" is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a brief definition that explains a trigger as an event that activates a workflow.

**Trigger Types:**

*   **Form Submission:** When a lead fills out any form on your landing page to request information or a resource.
*   **Tag Added or Removed:** When a contact receives or loses a specific tag, often used to start nurture sequences.
*   **Appointment Booked:** When someone books, confirms, cancels, or no-shows a scheduled call on your calendar.
*   **Email Event:** When someone opens an email, clicks a specific link, replies, or unsubscribes from your list.
*   **Pipeline Stage Changed:** When a contact moves to a new stage in your sales pipeline (e.g., from "New Lead" to "Booked").
*   **Date or Time Based:** On a specific calendar date, a contact's birthday, or a set number of days before/after an event.

**The Golden Rule of Triggers:**

*   Every workflow starts with exactly ONE trigger. Without a trigger, the workflow never fires.

**Key Takeaway:**

*   The trigger defines WHO enters the workflow and WHEN.

In summary, the image effectively illustrates the various types of triggers that can initiate automation workflows, emphasizing the importance of a single trigger for each workflow and its role in determining who enters the workflow and when.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the actions that can be automated in GoHighLevel, a marketing automation platform. The title, "Actions: What the Automation Does," is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a brief description of what an action is and how it works.

**Actions:**

*   **Send Email**: Deliver a specific email template or custom message to the contact immediately.
*   **Send SMS**: Send a text message to the contact's mobile number via LC Phone or Twilio.
*   **Add or Remove Tag**: Label the contact to segment them, track behavior, or trigger subsequent workflows.
*   **Wait**: Pause the workflow for a set time (e.g., 2 days) or until a specific event occurs.
*   **Move to Pipeline**: Create, update, or advance an opportunity card in your visual sales pipeline.
*   **Create Task**: Assign a manual follow-up task (e.g., "Call Lead") to a specific team member.
*   **Internal Notification**: Alert you or your team via email/app when a lead takes a key action.
*   **Webhook**: Send contact data to an external tool like Zapier, Slack, or another CRM.

**The Power of Actions:**

*   Actions are what automation actually DOES. Without actions, the workflow fires but nothing happens.

**Key Takeaway:**

*   The action sequence defines the COMPLETE EXPERIENCE your contact receives.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise explanation of the various actions that can be automated in GoHighLevel, highlighting their importance in defining the complete experience for contacts. By understanding these actions, users can create effective automation workflows that streamline their marketing efforts and improve customer engagement.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the actions that can be automated in GoHighLevel, a marketing automation platform. The title "Actions: What the Automation Does" is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a brief description of what actions are and how they work.

**Main Points:**

* **Actions:** An action is a task GoHighLevel performs automatically after a trigger fires.
* **Action Types:** The image highlights eight different types of actions that can be automated:
	+ Send Email
	+ Send SMS
	+ Add or Remove Tag
	+ Wait
	+ Move to Pipeline
	+ Create Task
	+ Internal Notification
	+ Webhook
* **Action Descriptions:** Each action type is accompanied by a brief description explaining its purpose and functionality.
* **The Power of Actions:** A section at the bottom of the image emphasizes the importance of actions, stating that they are what automation actually does, and without them, the workflow fires but nothing happens.
* **Complete Experience:** A call-to-action at the bottom of the image encourages users to define their complete experience by determining the action sequence.

**Summary:**

The image provides a clear and concise overview of the various actions that can be automated in GoHighLevel, highlighting their importance in creating a complete experience for contacts. By understanding the different types of actions available and how they work, users can effectively leverage automation to streamline their workflows and improve their marketing efforts.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to understanding conditions in automation, specifically how they facilitate decision-making processes. The visual content is organized into clear sections, making it easy to follow and comprehend.

* **Title and Subtitle**
	+ Title: "Conditions: How Automation Makes Decisions"
	+ Subtitle: "A condition checks something about the contact and routes them down a different path based on the answer"
* **Flowchart**
	+ A flowchart illustrating the decision-making process with conditions
	+ The flowchart consists of:
		- A purple diamond representing the condition
		- Two lines extending from the diamond:
			- Green line labeled "YES Path" leading to "Take this action"
			- Red line labeled "NO Path" leading to "Take this other action"
* **Real-World Examples**
	+ Three examples of real-world conditions:
		- Did the contact open the last email?
			- YES: Send the offer email
			- NO: Send an SMS reminder instead
		- Does contact have tag 'High Interest'?
			- YES: Notify sales team immediately
			- NO: Continue nurture sequence
		- Has the contact booked a call?
			- YES: Send confirmation & prep info
			- NO: Send booking invitation again
* **Why Conditions Matter**
	+ A green banner highlighting the importance of conditions
	+ Text: "Without conditions, every contact gets the same experience. With conditions, your automation adapts to each individual."

In summary, the image effectively illustrates the concept of conditions in automation, showcasing their role in enabling personalized experiences for contacts. By using a clear flowchart and real-world examples, the image provides a concise and informative guide to understanding how conditions work and their significance in automation.

The image presents a screenshot of the GoHighLevel Workflow Builder, specifically showcasing an "If/Else" condition block. The title "GoHighLevel Workflow Builder" is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a green bar and the word "Libre" in the upper-right corner.

**Key Elements:**

* **Title:** GoHighLevel Workflow Builder
* **Subtitle:** Automation → Workflows → If/Else Condition Block
* **Workflow Name:** New Lead Welcome Sequence
* **Publish Button:** A green button located in the upper-right corner

**Workflow Diagram:**

The workflow diagram illustrates the following steps:

* **Initial Step:** Send Email (Day 3 Nurture)
* **Condition Block:** If/Else (Email Opened?)
	+ **Yes Path:** Send Email (Special Offer)
	+ **No Path:** Send SMS (Reminder Text)

**Annotations:**

* **Condition Block:** GoHighLevel calls this 'If/Else' - this is where the workflow checks something and branches.
* **The Check:** In this example: "Did the contact open the previous email?"
* **Yes Path:** Contacts who meet the condition go here.
* **No Path:** Contacts who do not meet the condition go here.

**Footer:**

A black bar at the bottom of the image contains white text that reads: "You will configure If/Else blocks like this when building advanced workflows in Lessons 7.3 and 7.4."

**Background:**

The background features a white grid pattern, providing a clean and organized visual representation of the workflow builder.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of five common automation patterns, each represented by a distinct color-coded box. These patterns are designed to facilitate the creation of workflows and are likely to appear in almost every workflow built.

* **Lead Welcome**
	+ Description: A green box with a hand icon
	+ Workflow:
		- Form Submitted → Send Welcome Email → Wait 1 Day → If Opened?
			- Yes: Day 2 Nurture
			- No: SMS Reminder
* **Appointment**
	+ Description: A blue box with a calendar icon
	+ Workflow:
		- Booked → Send Confirmation → Wait until 24hr before → SMS Remind → Attended?
			- Yes: Move Pipeline
			- No: Re-book Email
* **Segmentation**
	+ Description: A yellow box with a target icon
	+ Workflow:
		- Tag Added: High Interest → Notify Team → Send Offer Email → Link Clicked?
			- Yes: Move to Closing
			- No: Continue Nurture
* **Re-Engagement**
	+ Description: A pink box with a refresh icon
	+ Workflow:
		- Inactive 30 Days → Send Win-Back Email → Replied?
			- Yes: Resume Nurture
			- No: Final SMS → Still No: Tag 'Inactive'
* **Post-Purchase**
	+ Description: A purple box with a lock icon
	+ Workflow:
		- Stage: Won → Send Thank You → Wait 7 Days → Request Review → Submitted?
			- Yes: Tag 'Advocate'
			- No: Send Reminder

The image provides a clear and concise visual representation of five common automation patterns, making it easier for users to understand and implement these patterns in their workflows. By recognizing these patterns, users can build workflows more efficiently, as noted in the black text box at the bottom of the image, which states that users will build several of these patterns across Lessons 7.3 through 7.8.

The image presents a concise summary of key takeaways from a lesson on automation workflows, specifically highlighting the fundamental components and their definitions.

*   **Triggers as Activators**
    *   A trigger is an event that activates a workflow.
    *   Every automation starts with exactly one trigger, such as:
        *   Form submission
        *   Tag change
        *   Appointment booking
*   **Actions as Automated Tasks**
    *   An action is a task GoHighLevel performs automatically, including:
        *   Sending an email
        *   Sending an SMS
        *   Adding a tag
        *   Waiting a set time
        *   Notifying your team
*   **Conditions as Decision Points**
    *   A condition (also referred to as If/Else in GoHighLevel) checks something about the contact and routes them down a YES or NO path based on their behavior or data.
*   **The Complete Workflow**
    *   Workflows combine all three components:
        *   Trigger: defines who enters and when
        *   Actions: define what happens
        *   Conditions: make the workflow intelligent by personalizing the path
*   **Automation Vocabulary**
    *   Understanding these three components is the foundation for every workflow you will build from Lesson 7.3 onward.
    *   Triggers, actions, and conditions are the language of automation.

In summary, the image effectively outlines the essential elements of automation workflows, providing a clear understanding of triggers, actions, conditions, and how they come together to form a complete workflow. This foundational knowledge is crucial for building effective automations, as emphasized by the importance of understanding these components for future workflow construction.
